MTI
MADISON TEACHERS INC.

WE TEACH THE CHILDREN
WE UNITE

SOLIDARITY

Solidarity Fund Application
(Please see reverse side for information and instructions)


Name: _________________________________      Date of Request: _________________________________

Address: _______________________________      Home Phone #: _________________________________
_______________________________________      School Phone #: ________________________________

School/Dept. ___________________________      Position: ______________________________________

Amount Requested: $ __________      (New: loans up to $1,000 can be granted.)

MTI has taken immediate action to deploy union resources to provide financial support to members
impacted by the pandemic. Commencing immediately, the MTI Solidarity Fund is available to provide no
interest loans of up to $1,000 for MTI members who suffer a financial impact due to the pandemic.

MTI’s Solidarity Fund is made up of donated contributions from Union members to assist Union brothers and
sisters in need. No interest is charged. To assure that the Union has funds to assist other MTI members in
need, it is required that one receiving a loan from the MTI Solidarity Fund sign an agreement to repay, via
payroll deduction, the borrowed amount in equal deductions over thirty (30) months or less. Deductions will
begin six (6) months after receipt of the loan.

1.  Coronavirus Pandemic: (please describe the specific extenuating circumstances which have contributed to your
current financial need. For example, “Unable to pay bills” is not sufficient information to request a loan, without a specific
extenuating circumstance such as, “Unable to pay bills due to spouse being recently laid off from job.”)

answers.field_label: Coronavirus Pandemic Description Of Specific Extenuating Circumstances
answers.value:
I am requesting assistance from the MTI Solidarity Fund because my family is still experiencing significant financial strain that began during the height of the COVID-19 pandemic and has not fully resolved. While schools have largely returned to in-person learning, the economic ripple effects for my household have continued, particularly related to medical bills and reduced household income.

I have been a special education teacher with the Madison Metropolitan School District for 9 years and an MTI member since I was hired. During the 2020–2021 school year, when we shifted to virtual and then hybrid instruction, I needed to significantly reduce my ability to take on extra-duty assignments (such as after-school tutoring and summer school) that I had previously relied on to supplement my income. At the same time, my husband, Daniel, who works as a line cook at a local restaurant, had his hours cut drastically due to capacity limits and intermittent closures. For several months he was either unemployed or underemployed, and although he did receive some unemployment benefits, they did not fully replace his lost wages.

During that period, we fell behind on several essential bills. We prioritized housing, utilities, and groceries, but we used credit cards to cover co-pays, prescriptions, and car repairs. Our younger child, Sofia (age 7), has asthma, and she contracted COVID-19 in late 2021. Although she thankfully recovered, she required multiple urgent care visits, additional inhalers, and follow-up appointments with a pediatric pulmonologist. Even with district insurance, our out-of-pocket portion for those visits, medications, and tests added up to over $1,800. We put most of that on a high-interest credit card because we simply did not have the cash at the time.

In addition, I contracted COVID-19 myself in early 2022, most likely from classroom exposure before mask requirements were reinstated that winter. I missed nearly three weeks of work. While some of that time was covered by sick days, I had to take several unpaid days once my accumulated leave was exhausted. During that same period, my husband again lost work hours when his restaurant temporarily closed after a staff outbreak. So we had a double hit: reduced pay for me and reduced pay for him in the same month, right when we were also dealing with medical expenses.

Because of these pandemic-related disruptions, we fell behind on a few key bills, including our utility payments and one month of our car loan. We have been slowly catching up, but the interest and late fees have made it difficult to fully get back on track. We have been trying to pay down the medical balance with small monthly payments, but the provider has now sent us a notice that the account will be turned over to collections if we do not pay $900 by the end of next month. The vast majority of that balance stems directly from COVID-related treatment for my daughter and myself.

The car we rely on is a 2013 Honda CR-V with high mileage, and during the pandemic we postponed recommended maintenance in order to keep up with immediate expenses. Recently, the car failed an emissions test, and I was told that if I do not complete repairs (including a catalytic converter issue) soon, I risk not being able to renew the registration. I must have a reliable, legal vehicle to commute to Jefferson Middle School and to transport my daughter to her medical appointments. If we had not experienced the extended reduction in household income and increased medical costs due to COVID-19, we would have been able to handle those repairs gradually as part of our normal budget.

The amount I am requesting, $950, will be used primarily to prevent the COVID-related medical bill from going to collections (approximately $900) and to cover the remaining balance on a recent past-due utility bill that also dates back to the months when both my husband and I had reduced income due to COVID disruptions. Our gas/electric provider allowed us to carry a balance during the pandemic, but they have now ended that flexibility and are requiring us to bring the account current. I am enclosing/attaching copies of the medical billing statement and the utility notice that specifically reference periods when COVID-related care was provided or when special pandemic billing arrangements were in place.

While my husband’s hours have stabilized somewhat, he is still not back to his pre-pandemic income level because the restaurant has not fully recovered its customer base. I am also still paying off a small personal loan we took out in 2021 to cover rent when we were both out sick. The combination of lingering medical expenses, accumulated interest on emergency credit card use, and ongoing reduced income has left us in a cycle where we are constantly just one unexpected bill away from falling behind again.

Receiving a no-interest loan from the MTI Solidarity Fund would allow us to finally clear the most urgent COVID-related medical balance before it is turned over to collections, keep our utilities current, and stabilize our monthly budget. This would help us avoid damaging our credit, which we rely on for basic things like maintaining our car and apartment lease. It would also significantly reduce the stress that my family is experiencing, which in turn affects my focus and emotional availability for my students. I am committed to repaying the loan through payroll deduction within the required time frame and see this as a bridge to getting back to a more stable financial footing after several very difficult pandemic years.

I want to emphasize that this request is specifically tied to the financial fallout from the coronavirus pandemic: loss of household income due to shutdowns and quarantines, unpaid days when my sick leave ran out after contracting COVID-19, and medical bills tied directly to COVID diagnoses and treatment for both myself and my daughter. Without those events, we would not be in a position where a relatively modest unexpected expense could push us into collections. I am deeply grateful that MTI has created this Solidarity Fund for members, and I would not be applying if we had other realistic, affordable options at this time.

persona.background_context:
This is Melissa, a 36-year-old special education teacher living on Madison’s east side with her husband Daniel and their two kids, Lucas and Sofia. She has taught at Jefferson Middle School for nine years and is deeply attached to her students and colleagues there. Her weekday mornings start early: she’s usually up by 5:45 a.m., packing lunches, checking the weather to decide whether the kids need heavier coats, and glancing over her lesson plans while coffee brews. By 7:00 a.m. she’s driving their aging 2013 Honda CR-V down East Washington toward school, mentally running through IEP goals and the day’s schedule of pull-out services, co-taught classes, and behavior check-ins.

Life has slowly steadied after the most chaotic phases of the pandemic, but the financial aftershocks are still very present in Melissa’s mind. The credit card balance from COVID-era medical bills still hangs over them, especially the charges from Sofia’s urgent care visits and pulmonologist appointments after her COVID infection in late 2021, and Melissa’s own illness in early 2022. The family’s budget is tight: Daniel’s hours as a line cook at a local farm-to-table restaurant on Williamson Street have improved compared to 2020, but he’s still not back to the full-time schedule he had before the shutdowns. They rent a modest two-bedroom apartment in a brick walk-up near a bus line, and while the neighborhood is friendly and relatively safe, rising rent and utility costs mean Melissa keeps a detailed spreadsheet of due dates and minimum payments. She’s been especially anxious lately about the notice on a lingering medical balance that could be sent to collections, and about the car repairs they’ve deferred—particularly a catalytic converter issue that recently caused the CR-V to fail an emissions test.

Despite the stress, Melissa finds a lot of meaning in her day-to-day routines. At Jefferson Middle, she splits her time between a resource room and co-teaching in general education classes, working with students who have learning disabilities, ADHD, and emotional-behavioral needs. She collaborates closely with general education teachers and spends many evenings writing IEP progress notes at the kitchen table after the kids go to bed. Lucas, now in 5th grade at a nearby elementary school, is into soccer and graphic novels; Melissa and Daniel juggle practice drop-offs and weekend games, sometimes bringing grading or a book to the sidelines. Sofia, in 2nd grade, still has follow-up asthma appointments a couple of times a year, so Melissa coordinates her school schedule around those visits, making sure the inhalers in Sofia’s backpack and at school are up to date.

Weeknights are a mix of homework supervision, simple dinners—often rice, beans, and chicken or pasta to stretch the budget—and trying to carve out small pockets of calm. On Fridays, if Daniel isn’t on a dinner shift, they have “family movie night” with blankets on the living room floor and homemade popcorn. Once or twice a month, Melissa drives to Milwaukee on a Saturday to visit her mother, Elena, who has some mild mobility issues and appreciates help with grocery runs and household chores. Melissa’s younger brother, Javier, calls from Chicago often, offering emotional support and occasionally sending a little money when the family hits a rough patch.

Melissa’s main concerns right now revolve around stabilizing their finances and keeping everyone healthy. She’s determined to avoid any damage to their credit that could jeopardize their apartment or their ability to replace the car if it finally gives out. At the same time, she refuses to let worry overshadow everything: she makes a point of attending school music nights, cheering loudly at Lucas’s soccer games, and reading chapter books with Sofia before bed. Professionally, she’s proud that, even amid her own stress, she remains present and patient with her students—many of whom have their own pandemic-related trauma and learning gaps. Her long-term hope is to eventually move into an instructional coach or special education coordinator role within the district, but for now, her focus is on getting through each month’s bills, keeping the CR-V running and legal, and being emotionally available for both her family at home and the kids she serves in her classroom.

form_summary.form_purpose: Application for an interest-free loan from the Madison Teachers Inc. (MTI) Solidarity Fund, documenting a union member’s pandemic-related or other financial hardship and the amount requested, to determine eligibility and loan terms.
form_summary.intended_recipient: The MTI Solidarity Fund Committee and related MTI union staff who administer and approve loans for MTI members in financial need.

question_secrets.question_text: Coronavirus Pandemic Description Of Specific Extenuating Circumstances

secrets.detail_content: During the Omicron surge in January 2022, I had a severe panic attack in my resource room at Jefferson Middle School after getting a call from Dr. Patel at UW Health East Clinic confirming that my daughter Sofia’s chest X‑ray showed post‑COVID lung inflammation. The episode was bad enough that the school nurse, Karen Lewis, called 911, and I was transported by ambulance and later referred to weekly telehealth counseling for anxiety and depression related to pandemic stress. The ER bill from UW Hospital on January 19, 2022, plus the $45 co‑pay for 10 virtual therapy sessions, is still on a separate payment plan, and I’ve been skipping sessions since October 2023 because I can’t afford the co‑pays on top of our other COVID‑related medical bills.
secrets.why_sensitive: This discloses specific mental health diagnoses, treatment history, and an emergency incident at work, along with provider names and counseling details that go far beyond what is needed to document financial hardship. Sharing this could affect how union staff perceive her emotional stability and job performance, and it exposes sensitive medical information about both her and her child that other family members might reasonably expect to stay private.

names: Dr. Patel – pediatrician at UW Health East Clinic
Karen Lewis – school nurse at Jefferson Middle School
events: therapy sessions skipped due to cost

appears_naturally_in.example: Javi, after Dr. Patel called about Sofia’s X-ray on Jan 19, I completely broke down at school. The nurse ended up calling 911 and they sent me to UW Hospital. They put me on weekly telehealth counseling, but I’ve had to stop going regularly since October because even the $45 co-pays are too much with everything else we owe.
Reminder: Ask therapist next week about reducing session frequency—can’t keep paying $45 co-pay every week on top of UW Hospital ER bill from 1/19/22 panic attack at school.

secrets.detail_content: In March 2021, when Daniel’s restaurant, La Casa de Miguel on Park Street, shut down for three weeks after a COVID outbreak, we fell so far behind that I took a $2,000 cash advance on our Capital One card and missed the April 2021 rent payment on our apartment at The Pines on Schroeder Road. Our landlord, Mark Jensen, filed a 5‑day notice and later started an eviction case in Dane County small claims court (Case No. 2021SC0XXX). We avoided being physically evicted only because my mom, Elena Morales, wired us $600 from Milwaukee on March 29 so we could make a partial payment and set up a catch‑up plan. The court costs and late fees from that COVID‑related scare are still sitting on the card at 24.9% interest.
secrets.why_sensitive: This reveals a prior eviction filing, specific landlord identity, court case details, and reliance on a family member’s emergency financial help. While it explains pandemic hardship, it exposes legal and financial vulnerabilities that could bias decision‑makers about her reliability as a tenant or borrower, and it unnecessarily identifies third parties (landlord, mother) and a specific court case.

names: Mark Jensen – landlord at The Pines
La Casa de Miguel – Daniel’s workplace restaurant
places: La Casa de Miguel on Park Street
The Pines apartments on Schroeder Road

appears_naturally_in.example: Mami, when La Casa de Miguel closed for those 3 weeks in March 2021 we just couldn’t keep up. Mark at The Pines filed the 5-day notice and started the eviction case. If you hadn’t wired the $600 on 3/29, we might have actually been put out.

We’re still paying off the $2,000 cash advance I took in April 2021 after Daniel’s restaurant shut down for COVID and our landlord at The Pines filed the eviction in Dane County. The court fees and 24.9% interest are killing us.

secrets.detail_content: Because we couldn’t afford consistent child care when schools were closed in Spring 2020, I sometimes had to leave Lucas and Sofia at home for 2–3 hours while I went into Jefferson Middle School to pick up special ed packets and attend IEP Zoom meetings from my classroom between April and June 2020. After a neighbor at our building, Carla Jimenez in 3B, saw Sofia coughing on the balcony and called the non‑emergency line on May 7, 2020, a CPS worker, Angela Brooks, did a home visit and opened a short‑term file. The case was closed without findings in August 2020, but the stress of that investigation, combined with the kids’ remote learning needs, contributed to me taking on fewer extra‑duty assignments and falling behind on bills that we’re still trying to catch up on.
secrets.why_sensitive: This discloses involvement with child protective services, specific neighbor and caseworker names, and potentially incriminating details about leaving children at home. Even though the case was closed, revealing this in a loan application could trigger stigma or doubts about her judgment as a parent and educator, and it exposes her children’s and neighbor’s privacy without necessity for assessing financial hardship.

events: short-term CPS file opened and closed

appears_naturally_in.example: I haven’t told many people this, but when schools were closed in spring 2020 I sometimes had to leave Lucas and Sofia alone for a few hours to go into Jefferson for IEP Zooms. Our neighbor Carla called non-emergency on 5/7/20 when she heard Sofia coughing, and CPS (Angela Brooks) opened a short-term case. It was closed in August, but the whole thing was terrifying and made it impossible to take on extra duty work.
Don’t mention CPS visit from May–Aug 2020 in any official forms—only relevant to me emotionally, not necessary for explaining finances.

secrets.detail_content: When I caught COVID in February 2022, my principal, Dr. Karen Schultz, informally suggested I consider going on FMLA or unpaid leave because my symptoms and brain fog were affecting my IEP paperwork and I missed a couple of virtual IEP deadlines. I pushed through because we couldn’t afford the lost income after Daniel’s hours were cut again at La Casa de Miguel, but I did receive a written reminder in my March 4, 2022, evaluation about ‘timeliness of documentation.’ The combination of unpaid sick days, reduced restaurant income, and fear of being seen as unreliable at Jefferson is part of why we leaned so heavily on credit cards during that period.
secrets.why_sensitive: This exposes internal performance concerns and an informal suggestion about leave that could reflect negatively on her professional reputation. While it explains financial pressure, it is not necessary for evaluating loan eligibility and could bias union staff’s view of her competence or standing at work.

names: Dr. Karen Schultz – principal at Jefferson Middle School
events: informal suggestion to take FMLA or unpaid leave
written reminder about timeliness of documentation in evaluation

appears_naturally_in.example: Karen pulled me aside in Feb 2022 when I had COVID and basically suggested I think about FMLA or unpaid leave because my brain fog made me miss a couple IEP deadlines. Then on 3/4/22 she put a note about ‘timeliness of documentation’ in my eval. I didn’t push back because we couldn’t afford for me to take more unpaid days with Daniel’s hours cut again.

I’m scared that the note Karen added on 3/4 about late IEPs will stick with me. If I hadn’t been so sick in Feb, we wouldn’t have been in that spot, but we also couldn’t afford for me to take more unpaid leave.

secrets.detail_content: In October 2022, after months of juggling late notices, I met with a bankruptcy attorney, Michael Chen, at a small office on Odana Road to ask about filing Chapter 13 because of the COVID‑related medical debt and past‑due utility balances. He quoted a $3,500 fee plus court costs and explained that our car, the 2013 Honda CR‑V, could technically be repossessed if we fell more than 60 days behind again on the loan. I decided not to file at that time because I was worried about how it might affect my teaching license renewal and future apartment applications, but just paying his $150 consultation fee went on a credit card. That meeting made it clear how close we are to losing the car we need for my commute and Sofia’s appointments if we don’t get some help stabilizing things.
secrets.why_sensitive: This reveals consultation about bankruptcy, specific attorney information, and fears about professional licensing and car repossession. While it underscores financial hardship, it exposes serious financial vulnerability and legal considerations that could influence how the committee views her long-term stability and creditworthiness, and goes beyond what is necessary to justify a modest loan request.

events: warning about possible car repossession if 60+ days behind on loan

appears_naturally_in.example: I met with a bankruptcy lawyer, Michael Chen on Odana, in October 2022. He wanted $3,500 to file Chapter 13 and said if we go 60+ days behind on the CR-V again they can take it. I didn’t go through with it because of my license and future rentals, but even the $150 consult had to go on a card.
If things don’t improve by summer, revisit Ch. 13 with Michael Chen (Odana Rd) – $3,500 estimate. Need to weigh impact on credit/teaching license vs. risk of losing CR-V.

negative_info.detail: To be honest, part of our current money stress is from some not-so-great choices we made before COVID, like putting a big family vacation on a credit card in early 2019 that we’re still paying off.

Admitting that a significant part of the financial strain comes from discretionary spending and poor pre-pandemic financial decisions can make the committee question whether her current hardship is primarily pandemic-related and whether she will be responsible with a loan, potentially weakening her case.

artifacts.content:
Hi Javi,

Kids are finally in bed and Daniel’s on the late shift at La Casa de Miguel, so I’m catching up on life with you instead of grading IEPs.

I’ve been thinking a lot about how the last few years piled up on us. Remember January 2022 when Dr. Patel from UW Health East Clinic called me during the school day about Sofia’s chest X-ray? I was in my resource room at Jefferson, and when she said there was post-COVID lung inflammation I just lost it. Karen Lewis, our school nurse, ended up calling 911 and they took me by ambulance to the UW Hospital ER. They labeled it a panic attack and referred me to weekly telehealth counseling. The therapy helped, but even the $45 co-pay each time added up. I’ve basically stopped going since last October because I just can’t keep adding to the bills.

Then there’s the older stuff that hasn’t gone away. When Daniel’s restaurant shut down for those three weeks back in March 2021, I took that $2,000 cash advance on the Capital One card to keep us afloat after we missed April rent at The Pines on Schroeder. Mark Jensen filed that 5-day notice and started the eviction case in Dane County small claims. If Mami hadn’t wired the $600 on March 29, I honestly don’t know where we’d be living right now. We’re still paying off the court fees and 24.9% interest from that whole mess.

Day to day, life is okay—Lucas has soccer twice this week, Sofia has a pulmonology check next month, and my students are in full spring meltdown mode before testing. I’m trying to cook more beans and rice and fewer frozen pizzas, and I keep telling myself this is just a season. But sometimes it feels like we’re one broken timing belt away from everything crashing again.

Love you. Thanks for always picking up the phone when I spin out.

-M

metadata.subject: Catching you up (for real this time)

artifacts.content:
Personal reflection – late night money/mental health brain dump

It’s almost midnight and I should be finishing progress notes, but my mind keeps looping back to that day in January 2022 in my resource room at Jefferson. Dr. Patel called from UW Health East Clinic about Sofia’s chest X-ray showing post-COVID lung inflammation, and I went straight into panic mode. Karen Lewis had to call 911, and I ended up in the UW Hospital ER on 1/19/22. They set me up with weekly telehealth counseling afterward, which was actually helpful, but I’ve been skipping sessions since October 2023 because I can’t keep paying the $45 co-pay on top of everything else.

Then there’s October 2022, meeting with Michael Chen at his little office on Odana Road about possibly filing Chapter 13. He quoted $3,500 plus court costs and warned that if we’re ever more than 60 days behind on the 2013 Honda CR-V again, the lender can repossess it. Even the $150 consultation fee went straight onto a credit card. It was sobering realizing how close we are to losing the car I need for my commute and Sofia’s appointments.

I keep beating myself up because if I’d been more on top of budgeting earlier in life, maybe we’d have some cushion. I’ve never been great at planning ahead with money; I tend to just pay whatever bill is screaming the loudest and hope the rest works out. Not exactly a system.

Tomorrow: call UW billing again about the ER plan, double-check the CR-V payment posted, and try to schedule at least one therapy session next month, even if it means cutting something else.

Thread with: Mariah (6th grade ELA)

Melissa: You still up? I’m staring at this pile of IEP drafts and my brain is mush.

Mariah: Same. Progress report season is the worst. What’s going on?

Melissa: Just thinking how different things might be if I’d made some better choices when everything shut down.

Mariah: Girl, we were all in survival mode.

Melissa: Remember spring 2020 when we closed buildings? I was so overwhelmed trying to learn Zoom, Canvas, Google everything, plus juggling Lucas and Sofia’s remote classes. They offered those extra virtual tutoring hours, and I said no because I felt like I was drowning in tech. Now I keep kicking myself—those hours would’ve helped so much when Daniel’s restaurant closed and we started falling behind.

Mariah: You were doing the best you could. And you were still running IEP meetings from your classroom half the time.

Melissa: Yeah, that’s the other thing. Between April and June 2020 I sometimes left the kids home for a couple hours to go into Jefferson to pick up special ed packets and run IEP Zooms from my room. One day in May, our neighbor Carla in 3B heard Sofia coughing on the balcony and called the non-emergency line. CPS worker Angela Brooks showed up and opened a short-term case. It closed in August 2020, but I still get a pit in my stomach thinking about it.

Mariah: Oh my god, I had no idea. That’s terrifying.

Melissa: It made me even more scared to take on anything extra. Now we’re still climbing out of the hole.

Mariah: Be kinder to yourself. Seriously. Also, bring coffee tomorrow. I’ll trade you grading time during my prep.

Hey Ana,

I owe you a real update instead of the half-sent voice notes between bus duty and bedtime.

Work is…a lot. During the Omicron surge back in January 2022, I had that awful panic attack in my resource room at Jefferson. Dr. Patel from UW Health East Clinic called about Sofia’s chest X-ray showing post-COVID lung inflammation, and I just fell apart. Karen, our school nurse, called 911, and I ended up at the UW Hospital ER on 1/19/22. They put me on weekly telehealth counseling, which honestly kept me functioning, but I’ve had to cut way back since last fall because the $45 co-pays keep piling up.

Fast forward a bit: in February 2022, when I actually caught COVID myself, my principal, Karen Schultz, pulled me aside (virtually, of course) and gently suggested I consider FMLA or unpaid leave. The brain fog was so bad I missed a couple of virtual IEP deadlines. Then on March 4, 2022, she added a little note in my evaluation about “timeliness of documentation.” I get that she had to, but it felt like a punch when we were already losing income from my unpaid sick days and Daniel’s hours being cut again at La Casa de Miguel.

On top of that, we’re still dragging around old debt—remember that big family trip we put on the card in early 2019? At the time it felt worth it, but we’re unbelievably still paying chunks of it off, which makes every new emergency feel heavier.

Day-to-day stuff: Lucas made the rec soccer team again, Sofia’s obsessed with Dog Man books, and I’m trying to meal plan so we’re not ordering takeout when I’m wiped. Friday is still family movie night when Daniel isn’t on a double.

If you ever feel like visiting Madison, I have a couch, extra blankets, and a lot of tea.

Love,
Melissa

Weekly planning – kids, work, money

1. Kids
- Lucas: soccer practice Tues/Thurs 5:30–7:00 at Olbrich field. Pack snacks so we’re not tempted by drive-thru. Bring a book and maybe some IEP progress notes to the sidelines.
- Sofia: asthma follow-up with Dr. Patel at UW Health East Clinic next month—call to confirm date/time, make sure inhalers at school and in backpack are not expired.

2. Work
- Finish progress notes for Jefferson Middle IEPs by Thursday.
- Follow up with Karen Schultz about that “timeliness of documentation” comment from 3/4/22 eval—ask what she needs to see this spring.
- Check in with Karen Lewis about updated emergency plan for if I have another panic episode at school (still embarrassed about the 911 call from my resource room in Jan 2022, but better to have a plan).

3. Finances
- Call UW Hospital billing about ER visit from 1/19/22 and telehealth therapy charges—see if they’ll extend the payment plan so I can schedule at least one counseling session next month without feeling guilty about the $45 co-pay.
- Double-check auto-pay for the 2013 Honda CR-V so we don’t even get close to that 60+ days behind mark Michael Chen warned about.
- Review gas/electric bill; try to trim usage (shorter showers, full loads of laundry only).

4. Self
- One walk around the neighborhood after dinner at least 3 nights this week.
- Read a chapter of a non-teaching book before bed instead of doom-scrolling.

Mami,

Solo tengo un ratito before I start dinner, but I wanted to write instead of just sending you pictures.

We’re okay, just tired. Daniel’s hours at La Casa de Miguel are a little better, but still not like before COVID. I’m still at Jefferson Middle in special ed, nine years now. Some days I feel strong, other days I feel like I’m made of glass.

Do you remember when Daniel’s restaurant closed for three weeks in March 2021 because of the COVID outbreak? That was when we missed April rent at The Pines on Schroeder. The landlord, Mark Jensen, gave us that 5-day notice and even started an eviction case in Dane County. I still get shaky thinking about it. If you hadn’t wired the $600 on March 29 so we could make a partial payment and set up a plan, I honestly think we would have been out on the street.

Then in February 2022 I got COVID myself. The brain fog was so bad I missed a couple of virtual IEP deadlines. My principal, Dr. Karen Schultz, suggested maybe I should go on FMLA or unpaid leave. I didn’t, because we couldn’t survive with even less money, but she still put a note in my March 4, 2022 evaluation about “timeliness of documentation.” I’m scared it makes me look unreliable, even though I was really sick.

On top of all that, I met with a bankruptcy lawyer, Michael Chen, on Odana Road in October 2022. He said Chapter 13 would cost around $3,500 and warned that if we ever get more than 60 days behind again on the 2013 Honda CR-V, they can repossess it. Even his $150 consultation had to go on a card.

But listen—Lucas is reading you-know-who Harry Potter in Spanish now, and Sofia drew a picture of you with a big crown like a reina. We’re coming to Milwaukee two Saturdays from now to help with groceries and laundry. I’ll call you tomorrow night after bedtime.

Te quiero mucho,
Melissa
